2.6.a.info:
An autonomous AP is a standalone wireless access point that runs a full Cisco IOS image and handles every wireless function locally on the device itself. There is no wireless LAN controller (WLC), no CAPWAP tunnel, and no centralized brain — each AP is a complete, self-contained wireless system that you configure individually via CLI (console/SSH/Telnet) or its web GUI.

What lives on the AP: RF transmit/receive (obviously), client association and authentication (local user database, or per-AP RADIUS config), encryption and decryption (WPA2/WPA3 keys handled locally), VLAN-to-SSID mapping (each SSID tied to a VLAN that the switch must trunk to the AP), QoS classification and marking, and local RF tuning (channel and transmit power set per-AP, not coordinated across the network).

Why it doesn't scale: Because every function lives on the AP, every configuration change must be applied to every AP individually. Changing one SSID's pre-shared key on a 50-AP network means 50 separate logins, 50 identical config edits, and 50 chances to fat-finger a typo. There is no coordinated roaming, no automatic rogue detection across sites, no centralized monitoring, and no single-pane-of-glass view of the wireless network. Cisco officially positions autonomous APs for small deployments of roughly 1-3 APs, typically in a small branch office or home lab scenario where the overhead of deploying a WLC is not justified.

Modern status: Cisco still sells autonomous-capable APs (you can convert many Aironet and Catalyst APs between autonomous and lightweight mode using firmware), but essentially all enterprise deployments today use lightweight + WLC or cloud-managed (Meraki) architectures. Autonomous APs survive mostly as a CCNA concept and a legacy deployment pattern.

hack.memory: Autonomous AP = 'solo freelancer with their own IOS.' Everything lives on the AP — RF, auth, encryption, VLAN mapping, QoS, RF tuning. No WLC, no CAPWAP, no central brain. 50 APs = 50 logins. Use only for tiny deployments (1-3 APs). If the exam says 'standalone AP with full IOS' or 'no controller' = autonomous.
hack.practice: Flashcard drill (Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56): list the 6 functions an autonomous AP handles locally (RF, auth, encryption, VLAN/SSID mapping, QoS, local RF settings). Then list why it doesn't scale (per-device config, no centralized roaming, no RRM, no coordinated rogue detection). No lab needed — this is concept recall. Bonus: research the 'autonomous-to-lightweight conversion' process on Cisco.com to see why modern APs ship in lightweight mode by default.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56 (Wireless Architectures) introduces autonomous vs lightweight. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25 covers autonomous APs in detail. Exam question pattern: 'which AP type runs a full IOS and operates without a controller?' → autonomous. Expect 1 question on the architectural distinction.

2.6.a.info:
An autonomous AP must connect to the switch on a trunk port (IEEE 802.1Q), not an access port. The reason is architectural: because the AP handles VLAN-to-SSID mapping locally, it must be able to receive and transmit frames from multiple VLANs on a single physical uplink. Only a trunk carries multiple VLANs.

How traffic flows: A client on SSID Corp sends a frame to the AP. The AP decrypts it, looks up the SSID-to-VLAN mapping (say SSID Corp → VLAN 10), adds an 802.1Q tag for VLAN 10, and forwards the tagged frame out its uplink. A different client on SSID Guest → VLAN 20 sends a frame: the AP tags it VLAN 20 and sends it on the same uplink. The switch on the other end sees two different VLANs on one trunk and forwards each to the correct VLAN's Layer 3 gateway. If the switch port were an access port, all frames would be untagged and dumped into the same VLAN — SSID separation would be destroyed.

Native VLAN consideration: The AP's own management IP and CAPWAP-like control traffic (for autonomous, just management SSH/SNMP) typically lives in the native VLAN of the trunk (untagged). Best practice: set the native VLAN to something other than the default VLAN 1 for security, and ensure both switch and AP agree on the native VLAN number (mismatch = control plane breaks).

Contrast with lightweight APs: A lightweight AP's switch port is usually a simple access port in the management VLAN, because all client data is tunneled to the WLC inside CAPWAP (UDP 5247). The AP doesn't care about client VLANs — the WLC places clients into the correct VLAN at the WLC's wired interface. This is a guaranteed exam contrast: autonomous = trunk, lightweight = access.

hack.memory: Autonomous AP = TRUNK port. Lightweight AP = ACCESS port. Why? Autonomous handles VLANs locally so it needs to tag multiple VLANs on its uplink. Lightweight tunnels everything to the WLC via CAPWAP, so the switch port just needs one management VLAN. Mnemonic: 'Autonomous = All VLANs = trunk.' 'Lightweight = Lonely VLAN = access.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer with a generic AP: create two SSIDs mapped to VLAN 10 and VLAN 20. Connect the AP to a switch. Try it as an access port first → clients on one SSID work, the other doesn't, because only one VLAN comes through. Convert the switch port to a trunk with both VLANs allowed → both SSIDs work. This one lab cements why autonomous APs need trunks.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56 covers the trunk requirement. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. The exam loves this contrast: 'the autonomous AP is connected to a switch access port and Guest SSID clients cannot reach the internet — why?' Answer: the access port only passes one VLAN, so the other SSID's traffic is being dropped or lumped into the wrong VLAN. Change to a trunk.

2.6.a.info:
Autonomous APs hit a management scalability wall very quickly — Cisco and most network engineers agree the practical ceiling is around 3 APs before administration becomes painful, and past 5-10 APs it becomes completely unmanageable for enterprise standards.

The math of per-device configuration: Every configuration change — rotating a WPA2 pre-shared key, adding a new SSID, changing a VLAN ID, updating a RADIUS server IP, applying a firmware patch — must be executed individually on each AP. With 50 APs, a single SSID password rotation is 50 separate CLI sessions. A single typo breaks one AP, and you don't know which one until users complain. Scripting helps (Ansible, Netmiko), but you're still maintaining 50 stateful devices.

Missing enterprise features: (1) No centralized roaming — each AP authenticates clients independently, so a client moving between APs must fully re-authenticate and may drop the connection. No 802.11r Fast Transition coordination. (2) No RRM (Radio Resource Management) — channels and transmit power are set per-AP, so APs don't coordinate to avoid co-channel interference. Two adjacent APs may both pick channel 1 and drown each other out. (3) No coordinated rogue detection — no correlation across APs to identify rogue APs by their signal at multiple known APs. (4) No centralized logging, analytics, or dashboards — you SSH into each AP to troubleshoot.

When autonomous is acceptable: A small office with 1-3 APs, no roaming requirements, a single SSID, a tech-savvy admin, and no budget for a WLC. Home labs studying for CCNA. A temporary pop-up site. Anywhere else: use a WLC.

right.label: WLC solves all of these
items: One config, pushed to all APs

hack.memory: Autonomous APs have a hard wall at ~3. Past that, you're hand-managing chaos. WLC-managed (lightweight) starts paying for itself at 5+ APs and becomes required at 10+. Think: 'autonomous = garage band (solo musicians, no conductor). WLC = orchestra (one conductor syncing everyone).'
hack.practice: Whiteboard exercise: list 5 routine wireless admin tasks (rotate PSK, add SSID, update RADIUS IP, schedule firmware upgrade, detect rogue AP). For each, write how autonomous APs handle it (50 logins) vs lightweight+WLC (1 WLC config). This side-by-side makes the scalability argument impossible to forget.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56 explicitly calls out the ~3 AP limit. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25 covers autonomous drawbacks. Exam framing: 'why did Cisco move to controller-based architectures?' → scalability, centralized management, RRM, coordinated roaming, rogue detection. Expect one 'when do you use autonomous vs lightweight?' question.

2.6.a.info:
Autonomous APs operate completely independently of any controller. They do not run CAPWAP, they do not establish tunnels to any WLC, and they do not depend on any central device to serve clients. All 802.11 intelligence — beaconing, client association, four-way handshake, encryption, QoS — runs end-to-end on the AP itself.

Traffic path: A wireless client associates with the autonomous AP. The AP authenticates the client locally (against its own config, or by proxying to a RADIUS server the AP is pointed at). The AP decrypts the client's frame, looks up the SSID-to-VLAN mapping, tags the frame with the appropriate 802.1Q VLAN ID, and forwards it out its trunk uplink. The switch routes the frame based on the VLAN tag. Return traffic flows back down the trunk, gets decrypted by the AP, and is transmitted wirelessly. At no point is any traffic tunneled to a controller — there is no controller.

Management protocols: Autonomous APs are managed like any other Cisco IOS device: SSH or Telnet for CLI, HTTP/HTTPS for web GUI, SNMP for monitoring, Syslog for logs, optional TACACS+/RADIUS for admin authentication. These are all standard IOS management protocols — no CAPWAP, no DTLS tunnels, no WLC discovery process.

Key contrast points the exam loves: (1) Lightweight AP establishes a CAPWAP tunnel to a WLC before serving clients. Autonomous APs do not. (2) Lightweight APs can't serve clients without WLC connectivity (unless FlexConnect standalone). Autonomous APs have no such dependency. (3) Lightweight APs receive their SSID/VLAN config from the WLC. Autonomous APs store their config locally in startup-config like a router or switch.

hack.memory: Autonomous = no controller, no CAPWAP, no tunnels. Client traffic goes straight from AP to switch via trunk. Management uses standard SSH/HTTPS/SNMP — just like a router. If you see CAPWAP, DTLS, UDP 5246/5247, or WLC in a question, the AP is LIGHTWEIGHT, not autonomous.
hack.practice: Draw two topology diagrams side by side: (1) Autonomous AP → trunk → switch → network (no WLC anywhere). (2) Lightweight AP → access port → switch → WLC → network (CAPWAP tunnels between AP and WLC). Label the management protocols on each. This visual contrast covers 80% of the architecture section on the exam.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question pattern: 'which AP type does NOT require CAPWAP?' (autonomous), 'which AP type can serve clients without any controller?' (autonomous). This is pure recall.

2.6.b.info:
A lightweight AP (LAP) is an access point that runs minimal firmware and depends on a Wireless LAN Controller (WLC) for nearly all configuration, policy, and management decisions. The AP handles only time-critical RF functions; the WLC handles everything else. This is the split-MAC architecture that defines modern enterprise Wi-Fi.

What the AP does locally (real-time, microsecond-sensitive): transmits and receives 802.11 frames on the radio, encrypts and decrypts frames at wire speed, sends beacons every 100 ms, responds to probe requests from scanning clients, sends 802.11 ACKs (must be sent within microseconds — no time to tunnel to WLC and back), and queues/prioritizes frames at the radio.

What the WLC does centrally: authenticates clients (against RADIUS/802.1X, or via PSK verification), enforces security policies (ACLs, client exclusion, peer-to-peer blocking), creates and pushes SSID/WLAN configurations, runs RRM (Radio Resource Management) to auto-assign channels and TX power across all APs, orchestrates seamless roaming, applies QoS policy (WMM profiles, bandwidth limits), and manages AP firmware upgrades.

Boot-up dependency: A lightweight AP cannot serve clients until it finds and joins a WLC. On boot, it runs through the CAPWAP discovery-and-join process: get IP via DHCP → discover WLC (DHCP option 43, DNS, broadcast, or static) → send CAPWAP Join Request → authenticate via DTLS → download configuration and (if needed) firmware image → enter operational state. Only after this entire process does it begin beaconing and serving clients.

Why this design wins at scale: One WLC config change propagates to 500 APs instantly. RRM coordinates channel plans across the whole site automatically. Roaming is handled by the WLC so clients keep their IP and session as they move between APs. This is why every real enterprise Wi-Fi deployment uses lightweight APs.

hack.memory: Lightweight AP = 'corporate employee' — the WLC is HQ telling it what to do. AP handles time-critical stuff (radio, encrypt/decrypt, beacons, ACKs). WLC handles policy (auth, SSID, RRM, roaming, QoS). Can't serve clients until it joins the WLC. Split-MAC = 'body (AP) does reflexes, brain (WLC) does thinking.'
hack.practice: Packet Tracer lab (Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56): drop in a WLC (3504 or vWLC) and a lightweight AP. Watch the AP boot — note it doesn't serve clients until it joins the WLC. Create a WLAN on the WLC, associate a client, verify traffic. Then shut the WLC interface — watch existing clients stay connected briefly (stateful CAPWAP has a holdtime) but new clients can't associate. This demonstrates the dependency.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 56-57 cover lightweight APs. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam questions: 'what protocol do lightweight APs use to communicate with the WLC?' (CAPWAP), 'can a lightweight AP serve clients without a WLC?' (No, unless in FlexConnect standalone mode). Expect 2-3 questions on this architecture.

2.6.b.info:
CAPWAP (Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points, defined in RFC 5415) is the IETF-standard protocol that lightweight APs use to communicate with a WLC. It replaced Cisco's proprietary LWAPP protocol as the industry standard around 2009.

What CAPWAP does: (1) AP discovery and join — the process of finding the right WLC and establishing a trust relationship. (2) Configuration distribution — the WLC pushes WLAN/SSID configs, radio settings, security policies, and QoS policies to the AP. (3) Firmware management — the WLC can push a new AP firmware image over CAPWAP. (4) Statistics and telemetry — the AP sends client counts, RF measurements, rogue detections, and health data back to the WLC. (5) Client data tunneling — in local mode, all client data is encapsulated inside a CAPWAP data tunnel and forwarded to the WLC for VLAN placement.

Two tunnels per AP: CAPWAP uses UDP transport and creates two separate tunnels between each AP and the WLC: Control tunnel on UDP 5246 (always encrypted with DTLS) and Data tunnel on UDP 5247 (DTLS is optional, usually off for performance). Both tunnels originate on the AP and terminate on the WLC's management interface.

CAPWAP is Layer 3 aware: Unlike older L2-only wireless protocols, CAPWAP can operate across routed networks. An AP on one subnet can join a WLC on a completely different subnet across the enterprise, as long as IP routing works and UDP 5246/5247 are not blocked by firewalls. This enables deployments where a central WLC serves hundreds of APs spread across many buildings and campuses.

hack.memory: CAPWAP = 'Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points.' RFC 5415. Successor to Cisco's proprietary LWAPP. Two UDP tunnels between AP and WLC: 5246 (control, always DTLS) and 5247 (data, optional DTLS). L3-aware — can cross routed networks. If you see a wireless question mentioning RFC 5415 or tunneling between AP and WLC, the answer is CAPWAP.
hack.practice: Memorize: RFC 5415. UDP 5246 = control. UDP 5247 = data. Control always encrypted. Data optional. Write it 10 times. Then in Packet Tracer, filter a CAPWAP session in simulation mode — you'll see the 5246 control exchange first (discovery/join), then the 5247 data packets as clients start sending traffic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57 covers CAPWAP in detail. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Nearly guaranteed 1 exam question on CAPWAP ports. Know: 5246/5247, control/data, DTLS-always vs DTLS-optional, RFC 5415.

2.6.b.info:
The CAPWAP control tunnel uses UDP port 5246 and is always encrypted with DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security — the UDP equivalent of TLS). There is no option to disable encryption on the control channel; it is mandatory by design because the control channel carries sensitive management information.

What travels over 5246: (1) Discovery messages — the AP's initial 'hello, where's the WLC?' broadcast and the WLC's response. (2) Join Request/Response — the handshake that establishes a trusted AP-to-WLC relationship, using certificates for mutual authentication. (3) Configuration downloads — WLAN definitions, SSID names, PSKs, RADIUS server IPs, VLAN assignments, ACLs, QoS policies. (4) Firmware upgrades — the WLC can push a new AP image over the control channel. (5) Client authentication exchanges — when a wireless client does 802.1X, the AP forwards EAP frames to the WLC via CAPWAP control, which then talks to RADIUS. (6) Statistics and telemetry — AP health, client counts, RF stats, rogue detections.

Why DTLS is mandatory: The control channel carries PSKs, client credentials, and AP certificates. If an attacker could see or modify these in flight, they could impersonate APs, inject malicious configs, or capture wireless keys. DTLS (the UDP version of TLS 1.2) provides confidentiality, integrity, and authentication using X.509 certificates. Cisco APs ship with manufacturer-installed certificates (MIC) that the WLC verifies during the Join Request.

Firewall implication: If you deploy APs across a routed network or through a firewall to reach the WLC, UDP 5246 must be permitted in both directions (AP-initiated). Block it and the AP can never join the WLC — no beaconing, no clients, no service.

hack.memory: 5246 = control = always DTLS. Remember: '46 controls (starts with lower number, more important).' The control tunnel must be encrypted because it carries PSKs, 802.1X credentials, and AP certificates. If a firewall blocks 5246, the AP can't even join — no clients served.
hack.practice: Build a cheat sheet: UDP 5246 = control = DTLS always = carries discovery/join/config/firmware/auth/telemetry. UDP 5247 = data = DTLS optional = carries client traffic. Test yourself: 'which port would you unblock first for AP onboarding?' (5246 — without it, no join). 'Which port carries 802.1X EAP frames?' (5246 — they're wrapped in CAPWAP control).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57 emphasizes CAPWAP port numbers. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Classic exam questions: 'what UDP port does CAPWAP control use?' (5246), 'is CAPWAP control always encrypted?' (Yes, DTLS). These are guaranteed points — drill until automatic.

2.6.b.info:
The CAPWAP data tunnel uses UDP port 5247 and carries actual wireless client traffic between the AP and the WLC. Unlike the control tunnel, DTLS encryption on the data tunnel is optional and is typically disabled in production for performance reasons.

What travels over 5247: Every frame a wireless client sends — web browsing, email, video calls, file downloads, voice, anything. When a client on SSID Corp sends a frame, the AP decrypts the 802.11 encryption, then re-encapsulates the Layer 2 Ethernet frame inside a CAPWAP data header, and tunnels it to the WLC via UDP 5247. The WLC strips the CAPWAP header, places the client's traffic into the correct VLAN at the WLC's wired interface, and forwards it onto the wired network.

Why it's tunneled (local mode): In the default local mode, the WLC is the logical 'wire' for all wireless clients. The AP could be on any subnet anywhere in the network, but clients still land on whatever VLAN the WLC places them on. This enables seamless roaming — a client moves between APs on different subnets, but its IP stays the same because all its traffic exits through the WLC.

Why DTLS is optional on the data tunnel: Client data is usually already encrypted end-to-end (HTTPS, VPN, IPsec, TLS) or already encrypted at the 802.11 layer by the AP before reaching the client. Adding DTLS to every client packet doubles encryption overhead for marginal security gain. Most production deployments leave data DTLS off, unless they're running untrusted traffic over the CAPWAP path (rare).

Firewall implication: Like 5246, UDP 5247 must be permitted between the AP and the WLC. If 5246 is allowed but 5247 is blocked, the AP can join and show up in the WLC's list of managed APs, but no client traffic will flow — users will see 'connected' but no internet. This is a classic troubleshooting scenario.

layers: Client L2 Ethernet frame (to corporate network)

hack.memory: 5247 = data = DTLS optional (usually off). Remember: '47 delivers data (higher number, bulkier payload).' Client traffic is wrapped in CAPWAP and tunneled to the WLC. Classic troubleshooting: AP joins fine but clients get no internet? → 5247 blocked on the firewall.
hack.practice: Scenario flashcard: 'A lightweight AP is showing joined in the WLC. Clients associate and get an IP from DHCP, but can't reach the internet. Firewall admin says UDP 5246 is open. What's wrong?' Answer: UDP 5247 is blocked — control joined fine, but data tunnel is closed, so client traffic can't reach the wired network through the WLC.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam traps: 'which port carries client data?' (5247), 'is data always encrypted?' (No, DTLS is optional on 5247). Know the 5246 vs 5247 distinction cold.

2.6.b.info:
When a lightweight AP boots up, it has no idea where its WLC is. It must discover a WLC's IP address before it can send a Join Request. Cisco defines four discovery methods, tried in a specific priority order:

1. Static configuration (primed AP): If the AP has previously joined a WLC and saved that WLC's IP, it tries the saved IP first. You can also pre-configure a primary/secondary/tertiary WLC IP on the AP via CLI. This is the fastest path to recovery for a known AP.

2. DHCP Option 43: When the AP sends its DHCP Discover, the DHCP server can include option 43 in its Offer, containing a list of WLC IP addresses. The AP extracts those IPs and attempts to join each. Option 43 format for Cisco WLCs is sub-option type 0xF1, length, followed by WLC IPs in hex. This is the most common method in enterprise deployments because you configure it once on the DHCP server and every AP automatically learns the WLC IPs.

3. DNS lookup: If DHCP option 43 isn't set, the AP resolves the DNS name CISCO-CAPWAP-CONTROLLER.<local-domain>. The local domain comes from DHCP option 15. Create an A record for this name pointing to the WLC's management IP, and APs in that DNS domain will find the WLC automatically. Simpler to set up for a single-site deployment.

4. Layer 2 broadcast: The AP sends a broadcast CAPWAP Discovery Request on its local subnet. Any WLC on the same VLAN responds. This only works if the AP and WLC are on the same broadcast domain, which is rare in enterprise (WLCs usually live in a central data center subnet). Mostly useful in small deployments where everything is on the same VLAN.

Order matters: The AP tries them roughly in the order: broadcast (if a WLC is local), then primed/static, then DHCP option 43, then DNS. If any method returns a WLC IP, the AP sends a unicast CAPWAP Discovery Request, then a Join Request.

hack.memory: Four discovery methods: (1) Static/primed (AP remembers), (2) DHCP option 43 (most common in enterprise), (3) DNS name 'CISCO-CAPWAP-CONTROLLER.<domain>', (4) Local broadcast (same VLAN only). Mnemonic: 'APs SDDB — Static, DHCP, DNS, Broadcast.' DHCP option 43 is the enterprise standard. DNS is simpler for small single-site setups.
hack.practice: Configure a DHCP pool on a router in Packet Tracer with option 43 pointing to a WLC IP (format: 'option 43 hex f104<hex-wlc-ip>'). Watch a lightweight AP get an IP and immediately discover the WLC. Then remove option 43, create a DNS A record for 'CISCO-CAPWAP-CONTROLLER.example.com' pointing to the WLC, and confirm DNS-based discovery also works.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57 covers all four discovery methods. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam questions: 'what DNS name does a lightweight AP use to find a WLC?' (CISCO-CAPWAP-CONTROLLER), 'what DHCP option is used for WLC discovery?' (43). Know all four methods by name.

2.6.b.info:
The switch port connecting to a lightweight AP is typically configured as an access port in the AP management VLAN — not a trunk. This is the opposite of the autonomous AP design and is a favorite exam trap.

Why an access port works: In local mode, all client data traffic is tunneled inside CAPWAP (UDP 5247) from the AP to the WLC. The switch port doesn't see individual client VLANs — it just sees IP packets destined for the WLC's management IP, sourced from the AP's management IP. Everything is one VLAN as far as the switch is concerned: the AP's management VLAN.

Typical config:

interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 description Lightweight AP
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 10
 spanning-tree portfast
 spanning-tree bpduguard enable

VLAN 10 here is the AP management VLAN. The AP gets a DHCP IP in this VLAN, discovers the WLC, joins, and all subsequent client traffic is CAPWAP-tunneled over this same VLAN to the WLC.

Exceptions where a trunk is needed: (1) FlexConnect with local switching — when the AP switches client traffic directly onto the local VLAN instead of tunneling to the WLC, the switch port must be a trunk carrying the client VLANs. (2) Some advanced deployments with multiple management VLANs. For a vanilla lightweight AP in local mode, access port is correct.

Exam contrast to drill: Autonomous AP = TRUNK (AP handles multiple VLANs locally). Lightweight AP in local mode = ACCESS (all client traffic tunneled in CAPWAP). Lightweight AP in FlexConnect local switching = TRUNK (AP dumps client traffic onto local VLANs).

right.label: Lightweight AP switch port (local mode)
items: All client traffic in CAPWAP tunnel

hack.memory: Lightweight AP in local mode = ACCESS port, management VLAN only. Because CAPWAP tunnels all client traffic to the WLC, the switch port never sees client VLANs. Exception: FlexConnect local switching = trunk (AP drops traffic onto local VLANs). Drill the contrast: Autonomous=trunk, Lightweight local=access, Lightweight FlexConnect local-switching=trunk.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: connect a lightweight AP to a switch, configure the switch port as 'switchport mode access' + 'switchport access vlan 10'. Verify CAPWAP discovery and client association works. Then change the switch port to a trunk — everything still works because the AP sits in the native VLAN. Now try converting a FlexConnect deployment with local switching — you'll find the trunk is required to pass the local client VLANs.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question: 'how should a switch port connecting to a lightweight AP in local mode be configured?' → access port in the AP management VLAN. Classic autonomous-vs-lightweight contrast.

2.6.c.info:
Split-MAC architecture is the defining design principle of the lightweight AP + WLC deployment model. It splits the 802.11 MAC layer's functions into two groups based on how time-sensitive each function is:

Real-time functions stay on the AP — these are operations where even a millisecond of delay would break Wi-Fi (802.11 ACKs must be sent within microseconds). Management/policy functions move to the WLC — these operations happen on human timescales (authentication can take tens of milliseconds and still feel instant) and benefit from being handled centrally.

The name: 'Split-MAC' refers to the fact that the 802.11 MAC sublayer — which normally lives entirely on a single wireless device — is now physically split between two devices (AP and WLC). The lower MAC (real-time radio operations) runs on the AP. The upper MAC (management, policy, authentication) runs on the WLC.

Why this matters architecturally: (1) The AP firmware stays small and lightweight because it doesn't need the intelligence for policy decisions. (2) The WLC can enforce consistent policy across hundreds of APs instantly. (3) RRM (centralized RF management) becomes possible because the WLC sees the full picture of RF activity across every AP. (4) Roaming becomes fast because the WLC maintains client state across AP moves — the client doesn't re-authenticate from scratch when it moves between APs.

Historical context: Split-MAC emerged around 2003-2005 as Wi-Fi deployments grew. It was initially proprietary (LWAPP), then standardized as CAPWAP (RFC 5415) in 2009. Every major enterprise wireless vendor (Cisco, Aruba, Juniper) uses some form of split-MAC.

layers: Upper MAC on WLC — Auth, Policy, RRM, Roaming, QoS
Lower MAC on AP — RF TX/RX, Encrypt/Decrypt, Beacons, ACKs

hack.memory: Split-MAC = 'body (AP) + brain (WLC).' 802.11 MAC layer is split: lower MAC (radio operations, microsecond-sensitive) on AP. Upper MAC (policy, auth, RRM, roaming) on WLC. Rule: 'happens in microseconds = AP, can wait milliseconds = WLC.'
hack.practice: Draw a Wi-Fi stack diagram: PHY (radio) → lower MAC (AP) → CAPWAP tunnel → upper MAC (WLC) → wired network. Label which functions happen at each layer. This single diagram covers split-MAC, CAPWAP, and the AP-vs-WLC division in one picture.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57 explains split-MAC in detail. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question: 'in a split-MAC architecture, which component handles [X function]?' — answered by the AP-vs-WLC function tables.

2.6.c.info:
The lightweight AP handles all real-time, latency-sensitive 802.11 MAC functions locally, because the microsecond timing of wireless radio operations cannot tolerate the round-trip delay of tunneling every action to a centralized WLC.

The full list of AP-local functions (memorize):

1. RF transmission and reception — the physical layer and lower MAC duties: driving the radio, modulating/demodulating OFDM symbols, CSMA/CA medium access control, managing the transmit queue per access category (WMM), handling retries.

2. Frame encryption and decryption — after the 4-way handshake completes (handled by WLC), the pairwise keys are pushed down to the AP. From then on, the AP decrypts incoming client frames (802.11 payload → plaintext) and encrypts outgoing frames at wire speed. This must be AP-local because a 10 Gbps AP can't afford WLC round-trips per frame.

3. Beacon frames — 802.11 beacons advertise the SSID, supported rates, security capabilities, and BSSID. APs transmit beacons every 102.4 ms by default (the '100ms' everyone rounds to). This is far too frequent to tunnel from WLC on every beacon.

4. Probe request/response — when a client scans for Wi-Fi networks, it broadcasts probe requests. The AP must respond within milliseconds with a probe response listing the SSID and capabilities. AP-local.

5. 802.11 ACK frames — every unicast 802.11 frame must be acknowledged by the receiver within a Short InterFrame Space (SIFS) — about 10-16 microseconds. No time to tunnel anywhere. The AP sends ACKs immediately on RX, and receives ACKs for its own TX.

6. Frame queuing and prioritization (WMM/QoS) — per-AC transmit queues for voice, video, best effort, background. AP-local so real-time voice frames can jump the queue instantly.

7. Data rate adaptation — picking the right MCS rate per client based on signal quality. Microsecond decision.

hack.memory: AP does the RADIO-TIME-CRITICAL work: RF transmit/receive, encrypt/decrypt, beacons, probes, ACKs, WMM queuing, rate adaptation. Mnemonic: 'APs Run Everything Below Paxton Acks' (RF, Encrypt, Beacons, Probes, ACKs). If it has to happen in microseconds, it lives on the AP.
hack.practice: Make a two-column flashcard with 10 wireless functions. For each, decide 'AP or WLC?' based on timing sensitivity. Functions that must complete in microseconds (ACKs, encryption, beacons) = AP. Functions that can take milliseconds (802.1X auth, policy lookup, channel assignment) = WLC. Drill until instant.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57 covers AP vs WLC function division. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25 includes a summary table. Exam question pattern: 'which device handles 802.11 ACKs?' (AP), 'which handles frame encryption?' (AP).

2.6.c.info:
The WLC handles all management, policy, and coordination functions that can tolerate millisecond-level latency and benefit from being centralized across the entire AP fleet.

1. Client authentication — when a client connects to an SSID, the AP forwards the authentication frames (Open System, WPA2/WPA3 PSK verification, or 802.1X EAP) via CAPWAP control to the WLC. The WLC either checks credentials locally, or proxies to a RADIUS server (ISE, NPS, FreeRADIUS). On success, the WLC pushes the pairwise keys down to the AP and adds the client to the BSSID.

2. Security policy enforcement — ACLs, peer-to-peer (P2P) blocking, client exclusion (kicking misbehaving clients off), URL filtering, AAA override, VLAN assignment per user (dynamic VLAN via RADIUS).

3. SSID / WLAN creation and distribution — an admin creates a WLAN on the WLC (SSID name, security type, RADIUS, VLAN mapping, QoS profile). The WLC pushes this WLAN config to every AP in the appropriate AP Group. One config change propagates to hundreds of APs instantly.

4. RRM — Radio Resource Management — the WLC's algorithm for automatically assigning channels and transmit power across all APs. DCA (Dynamic Channel Assignment) picks the best channel for each AP based on RF scans. TPC (Transmit Power Control) tunes each AP's TX power to balance coverage and avoid interference with neighbors. CHD (Coverage Hole Detection) identifies areas where clients have weak signal and suggests power increases. This is only possible with a central view of the whole RF environment.

5. Client roaming — when a client moves from AP1 to AP2, the WLC maintains the client's context (IP address, security keys, VLAN, QoS policy). The roam can be seamless because the client doesn't re-authenticate and doesn't get a new IP. With 802.11r Fast Transition (FT), roams happen in under 50 ms.

6. QoS policy — WMM profiles, per-user bandwidth contracts, bandwidth limits per SSID, call admission control for voice.

7. AP firmware management — the WLC stores AP images and pushes upgrades to APs on a schedule. One upload to the WLC = all APs upgraded.

hack.memory: WLC does the POLICY-AND-COORDINATION work: Authentication, Security policy, SSID config, RRM (channel/power), Roaming, QoS, Firmware. Mnemonic: 'WLC Always Sets Rules, Really Quickly, Forever.' If it involves policy, central coordination, or millisecond-tolerant decisions, it lives on the WLC.
hack.practice: Configure a WLAN on a real or virtual WLC: create SSID, security (WPA2-PSK or 802.1X), assign VLAN, set QoS, apply to an AP group. Note how one config pushes to all APs. Then change the WLAN name and watch all APs update. This hands-on experience cements 'the WLC is the central policy engine.'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57 covers WLC functions. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question pattern: 'which device enforces security policy in a split-MAC architecture?' (WLC), 'which device runs RRM?' (WLC).

2.6.c.info:
The entire purpose of split-MAC architecture is to enable centralized management at scale. It solves the fundamental problems that make autonomous APs unusable beyond a handful of devices.

Benefit 1 — one-touch configuration: Change an SSID's PSK on the WLC → every AP in the AP Group picks up the change in seconds. With autonomous APs this would be 50 separate CLI sessions.

Benefit 2 — RRM (automatic RF tuning): The WLC has visibility into every AP's RF environment. It runs algorithms to dynamically assign channels (DCA) and transmit power (TPC) across the whole site, avoiding co-channel interference and coverage gaps. Autonomous APs each set their own channel and power blind, which leads to interference and coverage holes in dense deployments.

Benefit 3 — seamless roaming: The WLC keeps each client's security context, IP address, and QoS state consistent as the client moves between APs. 802.11r Fast Transition lets clients roam in under 50 ms. Autonomous APs can't coordinate a client's state across multiple APs without external help.

Benefit 4 — coordinated rogue detection: APs continuously scan for other wireless signals and report them to the WLC. The WLC correlates detections across APs to identify and locate rogue APs. An autonomous AP sees only what it can hear from its own antenna — no correlation possible.

Benefit 5 — centralized analytics and troubleshooting: One dashboard for client health, RF stats, authentication failures, roaming events. No SSH-into-each-AP archaeology.

Benefit 6 — consistent policy: Security ACLs, VLAN mappings, QoS policies, URL filters — all defined once on the WLC and enforced identically everywhere. No drift between APs.

Benefit 7 — fleet firmware updates: Push a new AP image to the WLC, schedule the upgrade window, done. All APs updated consistently with minimal manual effort.

hack.memory: Split-MAC = enterprise scale. 7 big wins: (1) one-touch config, (2) RRM auto-RF, (3) seamless roaming, (4) rogue detection, (5) central analytics, (6) consistent policy, (7) fleet firmware. Autonomous can't do any of these past a handful of APs. This is WHY the industry moved to lightweight+WLC architectures around 2005.
hack.practice: Scenario: a company is growing from 3 APs to 30 APs. Write the top 3 reasons they should move to WLC-managed APs. Answers should hit: per-device config is unscalable, no coordinated RF management means interference, no seamless roaming for VoIP users. This framing turns split-MAC theory into a concrete business case.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56-57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam framing: 'what is the primary benefit of a controller-based (split-MAC) architecture over autonomous APs?' → centralized management at scale.

2.6.d.info:
FlexConnect (formerly H-REAP — Hybrid Remote-Edge AP) is a special operational mode for lightweight APs deployed at branch offices or remote sites that connect to the central WLC over a WAN link. FlexConnect solves two branch-specific problems: (1) WAN bandwidth is expensive, so tunneling every client packet back to HQ wastes money; (2) WAN links fail, and without FlexConnect, a WAN outage kills branch Wi-Fi entirely.

Two key capabilities:

1. Local switching: FlexConnect APs can switch client data directly onto local branch VLANs, bypassing the CAPWAP data tunnel. Client traffic never crosses the WAN — it goes from AP → branch switch → branch router → internet, staying entirely at the branch. This preserves WAN bandwidth for real WAN traffic. Local switching is configured per-WLAN — you can have SSID 'Corp' centrally switched (tunneled to WLC) for security reasons and SSID 'Guest' locally switched for bandwidth.

2. Standalone mode: If the WAN link to the WLC fails, FlexConnect APs can keep operating independently using cached configuration (SSID settings, VLAN mappings, security policies) and cached credentials (previously authenticated users can stay connected and re-authenticate). A standard lightweight AP would drop offline entirely during a WAN outage — FlexConnect keeps the branch running.

Typical deployment: A retail chain with 50 stores, each with 2-3 APs. All APs join a central WLC at HQ over MPLS/VPN WANs. FlexConnect local switching keeps customer Wi-Fi traffic local. Standalone mode ensures a store's Wi-Fi keeps working even if the MPLS circuit has an outage. One WLC serves all 50 branches — no branch-local WLCs needed.

Exam framing: FlexConnect = branch office resilience. Mode transitions between Connected and Standalone are the core concept.

hack.memory: FlexConnect = lightweight AP for BRANCH OFFICES. Two modes: Connected (WAN to WLC is up) and Standalone (WAN is down, AP keeps serving clients from cache). Two features: local switching (saves WAN bandwidth) and cached auth (survives outages). Solves 'central WLC but remote APs with unreliable WAN.'
hack.practice: Whiteboard the scenario: 50-store retail chain, 1 central WLC at HQ, 3 APs per store, unreliable MPLS WAN. Draw what happens when the WAN to Store 37 fails. Without FlexConnect: APs drop offline, customers lose Wi-Fi. With FlexConnect standalone: cached creds keep returning customers connected, local switching keeps POS terminals on Wi-Fi working with the local internet breakout.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57 covers FlexConnect. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question: 'which AP mode allows a branch to keep serving Wi-Fi clients when the WAN to the central WLC fails?' → FlexConnect.

2.6.d.info:
Connected mode is FlexConnect's normal operating state — the WAN link to the central WLC is healthy, CAPWAP control and (optionally) data tunnels are active, and the AP is fully managed by the WLC.

What's active in Connected mode: (1) CAPWAP control tunnel (UDP 5246) is up — WLC can push config changes, firmware updates, authenticate new clients via RADIUS proxy. (2) CAPWAP data tunnel (UDP 5247) is up — but may or may not be used for client data depending on the WLAN's switching mode. (3) The WLC has full visibility of the AP's clients, RF health, and rogue detections. (4) New clients authenticate normally — the AP forwards 802.1X EAP to the WLC, which proxies to RADIUS.

Switching modes (configured per-WLAN):

Central switching: Client data is tunneled to the WLC via CAPWAP data (UDP 5247), same as a regular local-mode lightweight AP. The WLC places the client on its destination VLAN at HQ. Good for: sensitive traffic that must go through HQ (corporate data, regulated traffic), guest traffic that must be inspected centrally. Cost: uses WAN bandwidth for every packet.

Local switching: The AP switches the client's traffic directly onto a local VLAN at the branch, bypassing the CAPWAP data tunnel. Requires the switch port to be a trunk carrying the local client VLANs. Good for: guest Wi-Fi with local internet breakout, IoT devices, branch-local applications. Saves: WAN bandwidth, reduces latency (no HQ round-trip).

Mixed: You can configure one WLAN for central switching (e.g., 'Corp') and another for local switching (e.g., 'Guest') on the same AP. Very common in retail and branch deployments.

left.label: Central switching
items: Client on WLC-assigned VLAN at HQ

right.label: Local switching
items: No CAPWAP data tunnel for this WLAN
Switch port = trunk carrying local VLANs
Good for guest Wi-Fi, IoT, local breakout

hack.memory: Connected mode = WAN up, WLC reachable, two switching choices per WLAN: central (tunneled to WLC, uses WAN) or local (stays at branch, saves WAN). Think: 'central = via HQ (expensive but centralized). Local = via branch (cheap but decentralized).' Most FlexConnect deployments mix both per SSID.
hack.practice: Scenario flashcard: 'A FlexConnect AP at a retail branch has two SSIDs: POS (Point-of-Sale) and Guest. POS traffic must go through HQ for security. Guest traffic should go out the local internet to save WAN bandwidth. What switching mode for each?' Answer: POS = central switching (via CAPWAP data tunnel to WLC). Guest = local switching (AP drops onto local guest VLAN, exits via branch router).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question pattern: 'in FlexConnect connected mode, where is client traffic forwarded?' Answer: depends on per-WLAN switching config — central (to WLC) or local (to branch network).

2.6.d.info:
Standalone mode is FlexConnect's failover state — the AP has lost CAPWAP connectivity to the WLC (WAN outage, WLC failure, routing problem) and is operating independently using cached information. This is the killer feature that makes FlexConnect essential for branch deployments.

What survives in Standalone mode: (1) Cached configuration — the AP keeps serving WLANs with their current SSID names, VLAN mappings, QoS policies, and security settings. Clients don't see any SSID changes. (2) Cached credentials — previously authenticated users can stay connected indefinitely. Their PMK (Pairwise Master Key) and session keys are cached locally on the AP. When they roam or reconnect, the AP can verify their cached credentials and let them back on. (3) Local switching continues — client traffic that was already configured for local switching still flows through the branch network to the local internet or local services. WLANs configured for central switching stop working because the CAPWAP data tunnel to the WLC is gone.

What breaks in Standalone mode: (1) New user authentication — if a user has never authenticated through this AP before (no cached credentials), the AP cannot authenticate them because it can't reach the RADIUS server via the WLC. New 802.1X users are locked out. PSK WLANs still work for new users because the PSK is cached. (2) Configuration changes — admin changes on the WLC can't reach the AP. (3) Central switching WLANs — any WLAN configured for central switching has no data path to the WLC, so those clients lose connectivity.

Recovery: When the WAN link recovers, the AP re-establishes CAPWAP tunnels to the WLC, re-syncs configuration, and returns to Connected mode automatically. Usually takes 30-60 seconds.

Classic exam trap: 'In FlexConnect standalone mode, can a new 802.1X user authenticate?' Answer: No — the AP can't reach the RADIUS server. Only users with cached credentials can continue.

states: Standalone (cached config, cached creds, local switching only)

hack.memory: Standalone mode = WAN down, AP flies solo. Cached config + cached credentials keep existing users going. Local switching keeps flowing. Central switching dies (no tunnel to WLC). NEW 802.1X users CAN'T authenticate (RADIUS unreachable). PSK users CAN connect (PSK is cached). Recovery is automatic when WAN returns.
hack.practice: Scenario drill: 'WAN to branch fails. Before the outage, User A was connected to SSID Corp (802.1X). User B had never connected before. User C has a Guest SSID (local switching, open auth).' What happens to each? Answer: A stays connected (cached creds). B can't authenticate (RADIUS unreachable). C can still connect (no auth needed + local switching still works).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam favorite: 'in FlexConnect standalone mode, which users can authenticate?' → only those with cached credentials. New 802.1X users = locked out. This is a guaranteed exam point.

2.6.d.info:
Local switching is the FlexConnect feature that lets an AP drop client traffic directly onto branch VLANs, bypassing the CAPWAP data tunnel to the central WLC. Configured per-WLAN. This is how FlexConnect saves WAN bandwidth.

How it works: The WLAN is configured on the WLC with 'FlexConnect Local Switching' enabled. When a client on this WLAN sends a frame, the AP: (1) decrypts the 802.11 frame, (2) looks up the WLAN-to-VLAN mapping (pushed from the WLC but cached locally), (3) adds an 802.1Q tag for the mapped VLAN, (4) forwards the tagged frame out the AP's uplink to the branch switch. The switch routes the frame based on the VLAN tag into the branch's local network. No CAPWAP data tunnel is used for this WLAN's traffic.

Switch port requirement: Because the AP is now tagging multiple client VLANs on its uplink, the switch port connecting to a FlexConnect AP with local switching must be a trunk, not an access port. The trunk must allow the AP's management VLAN (untagged/native) and all the local client VLANs the AP will tag. This is a major exam contrast: lightweight AP in local mode = access port, FlexConnect with local switching = trunk.

Benefits: (1) Saves WAN bandwidth — branch traffic never crosses the WAN. (2) Reduces latency — no HQ round-trip. (3) Traffic survives WAN outages — local switching continues in standalone mode. (4) Better user experience for local resources (printers, branch servers, local internet breakout).

Trade-offs: (1) Harder to inspect centrally — if you need to run all traffic through a central firewall, local switching defeats that. (2) More complex switching config at the branch (trunk + VLAN plumbing). (3) Client VLAN must exist locally at the branch.

hack.memory: Local switching = AP drops client traffic onto the BRANCH network directly. Saves WAN bandwidth. Requires TRUNK port at the switch (AP tags multiple client VLANs locally, just like autonomous!). Per-WLAN config. Traffic bypasses CAPWAP data tunnel. Classic branch design: Corp SSID = central switching, Guest SSID = local switching.
hack.practice: Lab/whiteboard: branch with FlexConnect AP, local switching enabled for Guest SSID (VLAN 30 at branch). Draw the path for a Guest client's packet: wireless → AP decrypt → 802.1Q VLAN 30 tag → switch trunk → branch router → local internet. Compare with central switching for Corp SSID: wireless → AP decrypt → CAPWAP data tunnel (UDP 5247) → WAN → WLC at HQ → corporate VLAN.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question: 'which FlexConnect feature keeps client traffic at the branch instead of tunneling it to the WLC?' → local switching. Also: 'what switch port type does a FlexConnect AP with local switching need?' → trunk.

2.6.d.info:
FlexConnect is specifically designed for branch office and remote site deployments where: (1) many small sites share a central WLC, (2) WAN bandwidth is limited or expensive, and (3) WAN availability is not 100% reliable.

Classic use case: retail chain — 50 stores, each with 2-3 APs. Deploying a local WLC at each store would be 50 WLCs to license, manage, and patch — expensive and operationally painful. Instead, deploy one central WLC (maybe a redundant pair) at HQ. All 150 APs across the 50 stores join the central WLC via MPLS/VPN/SD-WAN. Configure FlexConnect with local switching for customer Wi-Fi (saves WAN bandwidth) and central switching for POS terminals (security). FlexConnect standalone mode ensures each store's Wi-Fi survives if its WAN link drops.

Other common use cases: (1) Small remote branches of an enterprise (regional offices, sales offices). (2) Pop-up locations with temporary WAN. (3) Distributed field offices for a service company. (4) Franchises with a centralized IT team at HQ.

When NOT to use FlexConnect: (1) Single-site HQ deployments where all APs are local — use standard local mode. (2) Very large branches (50+ APs) where a local WLC makes sense. (3) When you need 100% centralized traffic inspection with no local internet breakout. (4) Sites with completely stable WAN where standalone mode is never needed.

Architectural decision framework: How many branches? How many APs per branch? How reliable is WAN? How much does WAN bandwidth cost? Do you need local traffic breakout? Answers steer you toward FlexConnect (many small branches, unreliable WAN, need breakout) vs central WLC per site (few large sites) vs Meraki (prefer cloud, zero-touch).

hack.memory: FlexConnect use case = 'many small branches, one central WLC.' Retail chains, distributed enterprises, franchises. Avoids deploying a WLC per branch. Relies on local switching (save WAN) + standalone mode (survive WAN outages). If the exam describes a 50-branch deployment sharing a central WLC → FlexConnect.
hack.practice: Design exercise: you have a company with 1 HQ and 20 small branches (2 APs each). Network engineer asks whether to deploy a WLC per branch or use FlexConnect from a central WLC. Answer: FlexConnect. Reasoning: 20 WLCs is expensive and complex; 1 central WLC + FlexConnect achieves the same functionality with much less licensing/management overhead, plus standalone resilience.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question framing: 'a company has 10 branch offices connecting to a central WLC over WAN links. Which AP mode is best for branch resilience?' → FlexConnect.

2.6.e.info:
Cisco Meraki is Cisco's cloud-managed networking platform — a complete product family (APs, switches, firewalls, cameras, IoT sensors, SD-WAN) managed through a single cloud-hosted dashboard accessible from any web browser. There is no on-premises WLC, no local controller, no VM to manage. The 'controller' lives in Meraki's cloud infrastructure (globally distributed across AWS and Meraki's own data centers).

How it's different from traditional WLC: (1) No on-prem controller — you never buy, rack, cable, patch, or upgrade a WLC. (2) Dashboard-only management — everything via a web UI at dashboard.meraki.com. There is no CLI for daily operations (there's a minimal local-status page and a limited API, but no traditional IOS/WLC CLI). (3) Subscription licensing — Meraki devices require active per-device licenses. No license = device can't be managed (it keeps running with last-known config, but you lose the dashboard). (4) Zero-touch provisioning — plug in a new AP, it auto-discovers the Meraki cloud over HTTPS, pulls its config, and starts serving clients without any manual on-site setup.

Data plane architecture: Despite being cloud-managed, client data is NOT tunneled through the cloud. Meraki APs switch client traffic locally at the AP or through the local network, similar to FlexConnect local switching. Only management traffic (configuration, telemetry, analytics, firmware) traverses the HTTPS connection to the Meraki cloud. This means client performance is not dependent on internet bandwidth or cloud latency — the cloud is purely the brain, not the data path.

Positioning vs on-prem: Meraki targets organizations that want simplicity and scale without hiring network specialists. Retail chains, K-12 schools, distributed SMBs, hospitality. Trade-off: less deep customization than Catalyst/IOS, but dramatically simpler operations.

hack.memory: Meraki = 'Netflix for networking.' Cloud-managed, subscription-based, browser dashboard, zero-touch. No on-prem WLC. Data is switched LOCALLY at the AP (only management traffic goes to the cloud). Perfect for distributed orgs (retail, schools, SMBs) that want simplicity over deep customization.
hack.practice: Sign up for a free Meraki dashboard account (Cisco offers free trial licenses for study). Create a network, add an imaginary AP via serial number. Walk through the dashboard to see how SSIDs, VLANs, firewall rules, and client analytics are configured without any CLI. This hands-on UI tour cements 'cloud-managed = dashboard-driven.'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57 mentions Meraki. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25 discusses cloud-managed architectures. The CCNA exam only tests Meraki conceptually: 'which architecture requires no on-premises controller?' → Meraki. 'What licensing model does Meraki use?' → subscription. No configuration is tested.

2.6.e.info:
Meraki APs communicate with the Meraki cloud over standard HTTPS (TCP 443). This is a deliberately simple choice — HTTPS passes through nearly any firewall without special configuration, unlike CAPWAP which requires UDP 5246/5247 to be explicitly allowed.

What goes over the HTTPS channel: (1) Configuration pull — the AP periodically fetches its current config (SSIDs, VLANs, security settings, firmware version) from the cloud. (2) Telemetry push — client counts, RF stats, signal strength data, application analytics, location/presence data, and heartbeats sent to the cloud for display in the dashboard. (3) Firmware download — scheduled upgrades pull the new image from Meraki's cloud over HTTPS. (4) Authentication proxy — if a client uses 802.1X, the AP can proxy RADIUS requests through the cloud to a cloud-hosted or on-prem RADIUS server (Meraki Auth, Cisco ISE, Azure AD, etc.).

What does NOT go over HTTPS to the cloud: Client data traffic. A user's HTTP browsing, video calls, file downloads — none of that traverses Meraki's cloud. It's switched locally at the AP to the branch network and exits via the branch's own internet uplink or backhaul. Meraki's cloud is purely a management and analytics plane.

Cloud outage behavior: If the AP loses HTTPS connectivity to the Meraki cloud (internet outage, cloud maintenance), the AP continues forwarding client traffic with its last-known cached configuration. Clients stay connected, SSIDs stay up, data keeps flowing. You simply can't make configuration changes or see real-time analytics until connectivity returns. Similar in spirit to FlexConnect standalone mode, but for a cloud-managed AP.

Firewall friendliness: Because it's just HTTPS (443 outbound), Meraki devices 'just work' behind most firewalls with no special rules. This is a huge deployment advantage over CAPWAP in multi-branch environments with strict firewalls.

nodes: Client data: AP → local LAN → internet (NOT via cloud)

hack.memory: Meraki ↔ Cloud = HTTPS 443 (management only). Client data is NEVER tunneled through the cloud — stays local, saves internet bandwidth. HTTPS means firewall-friendly (no special ports to open). If cloud dies, APs keep forwarding with cached config. Contrast with CAPWAP: CAPWAP uses UDP 5246/5247, data can be tunneled.
hack.practice: Compare diagrams: CAPWAP (UDP 5246 control + UDP 5247 data, data optionally tunneled to WLC) vs Meraki (HTTPS 443 for management only, data never tunneled, stays local). Explain why Meraki is 'firewall-friendlier' for multi-site deployments (HTTPS just works; CAPWAP needs explicit firewall rules).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question: 'how do Meraki APs communicate with the cloud controller?' → HTTPS (TCP 443). 'Is client data tunneled to the Meraki cloud?' → No, it's switched locally.

2.6.e.info:
Zero-touch provisioning (ZTP) is the Meraki deployment model where a brand-new AP, straight out of the box, can self-configure and join the network without any on-site IT involvement. The workflow eliminates the traditional 'stage device → console in → configure → ship to site' process.

The ZTP workflow: (1) IT admin logs into the Meraki dashboard at HQ and claims the new AP by serial number (printed on the AP's box or label). The serial number is registered to the organization. (2) IT admin configures the AP's intended network, SSIDs, VLANs, security, and firewall rules in the dashboard. (3) AP is shipped to the branch with zero pre-configuration. Anyone (non-technical staff, retail store manager) can unbox it. (4) At the branch, someone plugs in a network cable and power (or PoE cable). (5) The AP gets a DHCP address, connects to the internet, and phones home to the Meraki cloud over HTTPS. (6) The cloud recognizes the AP by serial number, assigns it to the configured network, and pushes the full configuration and firmware. (7) The AP comes online with the correct SSIDs, security, and policies within minutes. No console, no CLI, no truck roll.

Why it matters: For distributed organizations (retail chains, school districts, franchises), traditional AP deployment requires an IT technician to touch every device at every site. ZTP reduces deployment cost from 'send a technician to 50 stores' to 'ship a box to 50 stores, have the manager plug it in.' This is a decisive operational advantage.

Replacement workflow: If an AP dies, replacement is equally simple: ship a new AP, have someone swap it, claim the new serial in the dashboard, and the config downloads automatically. The old serial can be retired. No config migration, no backup restoration.

Contrast with lightweight APs: Lightweight APs require WLC discovery config (DHCP option 43, DNS, or static WLC IP) and typically a certificate trust. Meraki APs only need internet + DNS to dashboard.meraki.com — much simpler path.

states: Non-IT plugs AP in at branch

hack.memory: Zero-touch = 'claim serial, ship, plug, done.' No on-site IT needed. Non-technical staff can deploy APs. Huge win for retail/schools/franchises with many small sites. Workflow: admin claims serial → AP ships unconfigured → plug in → AP auto-joins Meraki cloud → pulls full config → operational.
hack.practice: Scenario: you're deploying Wi-Fi to 100 new Starbucks locations. Compare on-site effort for (A) traditional lightweight APs requiring a technician per store to configure CAPWAP, VLANs, SSIDs vs (B) Meraki with ZTP — barista plugs in the AP. Answer (B) saves ~100 technician visits = massive cost savings. This framing makes ZTP's business value unforgettable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question: 'what Meraki feature allows a new AP to self-configure when plugged in at a remote site?' → zero-touch provisioning.

2.6.e.info:
Meraki uses a per-device subscription licensing model — every AP, switch, firewall, and camera requires an active license to be managed through the Meraki dashboard. Licenses are typically purchased in 1, 3, 5, 7, or 10-year terms. No license = no dashboard access.

License types: (1) Enterprise — the standard tier for most APs. Includes dashboard management, firmware updates, standard analytics, RF management, and basic wireless features. (2) Advanced — adds premium features like enhanced analytics, air marshal (wireless threat defense), advanced location/presence analytics, and SD-WAN. Tier names vary by product line.

What happens when a license expires: Meraki offers a grace period (typically 30 days) before hard-enforcing expiration. After the grace period: (1) You lose dashboard management access — can't log in to change config, view analytics, or monitor clients. (2) APs continue running with their last-known configuration and keep serving clients — they don't stop working immediately. (3) However, you can't push config changes, run firmware updates, or troubleshoot from the dashboard. The device becomes stale. (4) Eventually (weeks/months) Meraki may fully disable management, at which point the device is effectively bricked until re-licensed.

Financial trade-off: Traditional CAPEX (buy a WLC + APs, own them forever) vs OPEX (subscription). For a 10-year ownership cycle, Meraki's subscription costs are comparable to or higher than buying WLC+APs outright. The value is in operational simplicity — no WLC to manage, no VM to patch, automatic feature updates.

Exam framing: Meraki = subscription. If a license expires, the device stops being manageable (but continues serving clients for a period). This is a commonly tested distinction from autonomous/WLC-managed (no subscription, you own the device).

hack.memory: Meraki = SUBSCRIPTION (per-device, multi-year terms). Expired license = lose dashboard access, but AP keeps running with cached config for a while. Traditional WLC = CAPEX ownership. Meraki = OPEX simplicity. Trade-off: ongoing cost vs zero operational overhead.
hack.practice: Compare 10-year TCO: (A) Cisco WLC 9800 + 20 Catalyst APs, no subscription, ~$50K upfront, ~5 hours/month admin time. (B) 20 Meraki APs with 10-year Enterprise licenses, ~$40K over 10 years, ~1 hour/month admin. Compute total cost including admin time. This frames Meraki licensing as 'paying for operational simplicity.'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question: 'what licensing model does Cisco Meraki use?' → subscription (per-device). 'What happens if the subscription expires?' → loss of management access (device eventually disabled).

2.6.e.info:
If a Meraki AP loses connectivity to the Meraki cloud (internet outage, ISP failure, cloud maintenance, DNS issue), it does not stop serving clients. It continues forwarding client traffic using its last-known cached configuration and only management/analytics features are suspended.

What survives a cloud outage: (1) All SSIDs keep broadcasting — beacons, probe responses, client association, authentication (if local or cached), encryption. (2) Client traffic keeps flowing — since data is switched locally (not tunneled through the cloud), client → AP → local LAN → internet works unchanged. (3) WMM/QoS, firewall rules, VLAN mappings — all cached locally on the AP, continue to apply. (4) Local RADIUS authentication — if 802.1X points to a local RADIUS server, auth still works. Only if RADIUS requires cloud proxy does auth break.

What breaks during a cloud outage: (1) Dashboard access — can't log in and change anything. (2) Real-time analytics — client counts, application analytics, location/presence data stop updating in the dashboard (but data is buffered locally and uploaded when connectivity returns). (3) Firmware updates — can't schedule or deploy. (4) Remote troubleshooting tools — ping from AP, packet capture, client kick, etc. all routed through the dashboard. (5) Some cloud-proxied auth flows — Meraki Auth, social sign-on, captive portal hosted in the cloud.

Recovery: When cloud connectivity returns, the AP syncs buffered telemetry, checks for config changes made during the outage (none, because you couldn't log in), and resumes normal management.

Comparison with FlexConnect standalone: Conceptually similar — both keep APs serving clients during management-plane outages. Meraki's cloud is the management plane; FlexConnect's WLC is the management plane. Both cache config and credentials locally. Meraki's model is arguably more resilient because the cloud is globally distributed (no single point of failure).

states: AP serves clients with cached config

hack.memory: Meraki cloud outage = AP keeps serving clients (cached config), you just can't manage from dashboard. Data plane NEVER depends on cloud — clients' traffic is always local. Think: 'Meraki cloud = brain, AP = body. Brain out = body keeps breathing, just can't take new orders.'
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'The Meraki cloud dashboard is unreachable due to an ISP outage. What happens to existing wireless clients?' Answer: they keep working — data plane is local, config is cached, auth (if local RADIUS or PSK) still works. 'What can the admin do during the outage?' → nothing in the dashboard; wait for cloud to return.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Exam question: 'what happens to Meraki APs if cloud connectivity is lost?' → continue forwarding client traffic with cached config; management suspended until cloud returns.

2.6.f.info:
Cisco DNA Center (rebranded to Catalyst Center in 2023) is Cisco's on-premises network management and automation platform. It is an appliance (physical server or VM) that sits at the heart of Cisco's Intent-Based Networking (IBN) vision. Unlike a WLC — which manages only wireless — DNA Center manages the entire Cisco network: switches, routers, WLCs, APs, and SD-WAN edges, from one dashboard.

Scope: DNA Center is an on-prem platform (contrast with Meraki, which is cloud-hosted). It runs as a cluster of physical appliances (e.g., DN2-HW-APL) in the customer's data center, giving you full control over the management plane and data residency. The API is REST-based (JSON), enabling programmatic integration with other systems.

Key capabilities: (1) Network discovery and inventory — auto-discovers all Cisco devices in the environment and maintains a living inventory. (2) Topology visualization — interactive maps showing devices, links, VLANs, and reachability. (3) Policy management — group-based access control using Scalable Group Tags (SGTs), microsegmentation, SD-Access fabric policies. (4) Provisioning automation — plug-and-play device onboarding, centralized firmware/image management, automated SD-Access fabric deployment. (5) Assurance — AI/ML-driven analytics, client health scoring, application performance monitoring, automated root cause analysis, predictive failure detection. (6) Integration with ISE for identity and TrustSec, SecureX for security, and ThousandEyes for WAN visibility.

Positioning vs WLC: A WLC is a wireless-only controller. DNA Center is a network-wide management platform that can manage the WLC as one of many devices. Think of DNA Center as a layer above the WLC. For a pure wireless deployment, you might use just a WLC. For a full enterprise with SD-Access fabric or complex policy needs, you use DNA Center.

hack.memory: DNA Center = Catalyst Center (2023 rebrand) = on-prem network-wide manager. Manages EVERYTHING Cisco (switches, routers, WLCs, APs, SD-WAN). Contrast: WLC = wireless only. Meraki = cloud-managed. DNA Center = on-prem, all infrastructure. The core of Cisco's Intent-Based Networking (IBN).
hack.practice: Cisco dCloud (free for partners/students) has DNA Center demo sandboxes. Spend 30 minutes clicking through the UI: discover devices, view topology, navigate the four tabs (Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance). This UI tour cements what DNA Center DOES vs just memorizing the name.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 60-61 (Network Automation) cover DNA Center/Catalyst Center. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam question: 'what Cisco platform provides centralized on-prem management for the entire network (not just wireless)?' → DNA Center / Catalyst Center.

2.6.f.info:
Intent-Based Networking (IBN) is a Cisco strategy and operational model where network administrators declare what they want to happen (the 'intent') in business terms, and the management platform translates that intent into low-level device configurations automatically. It is a shift from imperative networking (type every CLI command) to declarative networking (state the desired outcome).

Traditional (imperative) model: 'Go to switch 3, configure VLAN 20, trunk to switch 4, ACL permit 10.1.1.0/24 to 10.2.2.0/24, push QoS policy X.' You define every step.

IBN (declarative) model: 'Users in the Finance group can reach Finance servers. Guest users can reach the internet only. Voice traffic gets DSCP EF prioritization.' The platform (DNA Center) figures out which VLANs, ACLs, QoS policies, and switch configurations achieve this intent and pushes them automatically.

The four IBN pillars (Cisco's definition): (1) Translation — converting business intent into network config. (2) Activation — deploying that config to devices automatically. (3) Assurance — continuously monitoring the network to verify the intent is being met. (4) Optimization — closing the loop with AI/ML to detect anomalies and self-correct.

Why it matters: Enterprise networks have thousands of devices and complex policies. Human admins can't manually keep every device in sync with business intent as policies change. IBN shifts the admin's job from 'type CLI commands' to 'define business outcomes and let the platform execute.' This is the same transformation that happened in server operations with Kubernetes — declarative > imperative at scale.

DNA Center is Cisco's IBN platform. SD-Access is the fabric that IBN policies are deployed onto. Together they implement the IBN vision for campus networks.

layers: Intent (business outcome: 'Finance can reach Finance servers')

hack.memory: IBN = Intent-Based Networking. You say 'WHAT' (intent: Finance → Finance servers). DNA Center figures out 'HOW' (VLANs, ACLs, QoS, SGTs, device configs). Declarative > imperative at scale. Four pillars: Translation, Activation, Assurance, Optimization. Same paradigm shift as Kubernetes for servers.
hack.practice: Traditional vs IBN flashcard. Traditional: 'VLAN 20 on switch 3, trunk to switch 4, ACL permit 10.1.1.0/24 → 10.2.2.0/24, QoS DSCP EF for voice.' IBN: 'Finance users reach Finance servers; voice gets priority.' The IBN version is business-language; DNA Center translates. Practice framing requirements in IBN terms.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 60. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam question: 'what Cisco platform implements intent-based networking?' → DNA Center. 'What is the key difference between traditional and intent-based networking?' → declarative vs imperative.

2.6.f.info:
DNA Center's feature set is organized into four functional areas that Cisco calls the DPPA pillars: Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance. These are literal tabs in the DNA Center GUI. Memorize the acronym and what each pillar does — this is a guaranteed exam point.

D — Design: Create the network's logical hierarchy — global → areas → sites → buildings → floors. Define IP address pools per site. Create network profiles (AAA, DNS, NTP, syslog servers). Define device templates that combine a hardware model with a configuration pattern. This is the 'blueprint' pillar — you define WHAT the network should look like before any devices are touched.

P — Policy: Define group-based access control using Cisco TrustSec and Scalable Group Tags (SGTs). Instead of writing IP ACLs ('10.1.1.0/24 can reach 10.2.2.0/24'), you define groups ('Employees', 'Contractors', 'Guests', 'IoT') and policies between groups ('Employees → Servers: allow; Guests → anything internal: deny'). DNA Center + ISE translate group policies into SGTs applied at switch ingress, enforced at egress. This enables microsegmentation without IP renumbering.

P — Provision: Push configuration to devices and manage their software images. Plug-and-play (PnP) onboarding — new Cisco devices discover DNA Center via DHCP option 43 and auto-configure. Image management — centralized repository of IOS-XE images; DNA Center pushes upgrades on a schedule. SD-Access fabric provisioning — DNA Center orchestrates the IS-IS underlay and VXLAN overlay across all fabric switches.

A — Assurance: Real-time network health monitoring with AI/ML. Client health scores (0-10) track every user's experience. Application health monitors app performance across the network. AI-driven root cause analysis — when something breaks, DNA Center correlates events across devices to pinpoint the cause. Predictive insights flag issues before users report them.

hack.memory: DPPA = Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance. Memorize this acronym COLD. D = blueprint (sites, IP pools). P1 = group policy (SGTs, not IPs). P2 = deploy (PnP onboarding, image mgmt, fabric). A = monitor (AI analytics, client health, root cause). These are literal tabs in the DNA Center UI. Mnemonic: 'Dogs Prefer Puppy Adventures' or 'Design Policy, Provision Assurance.'
hack.practice: Write the 4 pillars on paper. For each, list 3 concrete things it does. Test yourself: 'where do you create a new VLAN?' (Provision). 'Where do you monitor client health?' (Assurance). 'Where do you define who can talk to whom?' (Policy). 'Where do you define the network hierarchy?' (Design). Drill until automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 61. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam question: 'what are the four main functional areas of Cisco DNA Center?' → Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance. Expect at least one question referencing DPPA.

2.6.f.info:
SD-Access (Software-Defined Access, SDA) is Cisco's campus network fabric — a software-defined overlay that replaces traditional VLAN/STP campus designs with an automated, policy-driven fabric. DNA Center orchestrates SD-Access; the two are tightly coupled.

The SD-Access fabric stack has four pieces:

1. Underlay: IS-IS — the physical network of switches runs IS-IS (not OSPF!) as the routing protocol. IS-IS provides IP reachability between every fabric switch. The underlay is simple and boring — its only job is to provide Layer 3 connectivity between fabric nodes.

2. Overlay: VXLAN — on top of the underlay, SD-Access builds VXLAN tunnels between fabric edge switches. Every user/endpoint frame is encapsulated in VXLAN (UDP 4789) and tunneled across the underlay to its destination edge. VXLAN provides Layer 2 mobility over a Layer 3 underlay — endpoints keep their MAC/IP as they move between switches.

3. Control plane: LISP — the Locator/Identifier Separation Protocol tracks where every endpoint is. When an endpoint connects to a fabric edge switch, LISP registers its location. When another endpoint wants to send traffic, the source switch queries LISP to find the destination's current location, then encapsulates the traffic in VXLAN to that target edge switch.

4. Policy plane: CTS (TrustSec) and SGTs — instead of IP-based ACLs, SD-Access tags traffic with Scalable Group Tags at ingress and enforces policy at egress. This lets policy follow users across the fabric, even as they roam.

Benefits: (1) Automated deployment via DNA Center (no manual underlay/overlay/LISP/CTS config per switch). (2) Seamless mobility — endpoints keep their IP across any fabric switch. (3) Microsegmentation — fine-grained access control without VLAN sprawl. (4) Consistent policy anywhere in the fabric. (5) Simplified troubleshooting via DNA Center Assurance.

Exam framing: CCNA tests SD-Access conceptually — know the four pieces (IS-IS underlay, VXLAN overlay, LISP control plane, CTS policy plane) and that DNA Center orchestrates it. No configuration is tested.

hack.memory: SD-Access = Cisco campus fabric. 4 pieces: IS-IS underlay (simple L3 routing), VXLAN overlay (tunnels for endpoints, UDP 4789), LISP control plane (tracks endpoint location), CTS/SGT policy plane (group-based access). Orchestrated by DNA Center. Mnemonic: 'ILVC' = IS-IS, LISP, VXLAN, CTS. Or 'I Love Valid Configs.' Endpoints keep their IP anywhere = seamless mobility.
hack.practice: Draw the SD-Access stack top-down: Policy (SGTs) → Control (LISP) → Overlay (VXLAN) → Underlay (IS-IS). Label each layer's role. Compare to traditional campus: VLANs + STP + routing, manually configured, no mobility. This contrast makes SD-Access's benefits obvious.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 61. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam question: 'what protocols make up SD-Access?' → IS-IS underlay, VXLAN overlay, LISP control plane, CTS policy plane. Memorize all four — this is a favorite exam point.

2.6.f.info:
The scope of DNA Center versus a WLC is a guaranteed exam distinction. The question is simple: what does each manage?

WLC scope — wireless only: A Wireless LAN Controller manages APs and wireless clients. It handles WLAN/SSID configuration, AP onboarding (CAPWAP), RRM (channel + power), client authentication for wireless, security policies for wireless, and wireless roaming. The WLC has no visibility into wired switches, routers, firewalls, or other network infrastructure. It is a wireless-only controller.

DNA Center scope — entire network: DNA Center (Catalyst Center) manages all Cisco network infrastructure from one platform: Catalyst switches, ISR/ASR/Cat 8K routers, WLCs (including the 9800), APs, ASR/ISR SD-WAN edges. It provides unified configuration, policy, provisioning, and assurance across wired AND wireless. It can manage the WLC as one of many devices in the inventory.

Relationship: In a deployment with both, DNA Center sits above the WLC. DNA Center pushes WLAN configurations to the WLC via APIs; the WLC pushes those to lightweight APs via CAPWAP. DNA Center provides the network-wide view; the WLC provides the wireless-specific control plane.

Analogy: WLC = department manager (runs one department — wireless). DNA Center = CEO (oversees all departments — wired, wireless, WAN, security). Both roles exist; one is broader than the other.

When to use which: (1) WLC only — small/medium deployments with wireless needs, no SD-Access, no complex policy. Catalyst 9800 WLC is the modern choice. (2) DNA Center — enterprise campus with SD-Access, IBN requirements, complex policy, multi-site networks, or a desire for unified management across wired and wireless.

Exam trap: 'Which platform manages both wired switches and wireless APs?' → DNA Center. 'Which platform manages only wireless?' → WLC. 'Which platform is the controller for an SD-Access fabric?' → DNA Center (the WLC participates as a fabric device).

hack.memory: WLC = WIRELESS only (APs, WLANs, CAPWAP, RRM). DNA Center = EVERYTHING (wired switches, routers, WLCs, APs, SD-WAN, security). Analogy: WLC = department manager, DNA Center = CEO. In a full stack, DNA Center sits ABOVE the WLC and manages it as one device. Drill: 'wired + wireless + policy + assurance' → DNA Center. 'wireless only' → WLC.
hack.practice: Scenario drill: 'A network has 50 switches, 10 routers, 5 WLCs, 200 APs, and requires group-based policy with SGTs. What platform?' → DNA Center. 'A network has 3 WLCs and 150 APs only, basic RRM and roaming, no SD-Access.' → WLC is sufficient. Practice picking the right tool for the described scope.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 57, 61. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 25, 29. Exam question: 'which Cisco platform provides centralized management for the entire network, not just wireless?' → DNA Center / Catalyst Center. This scope question is near-guaranteed.
